steps.text: Use trip or travel blanks to document contamination.
Include sampling location, time, date, and conditions in forms.
Perform field measurements such as pH and temperature on a separate subsample.
Conduct regular quality compliance checks and audits.
Describe the technique’s scope and applicability.
Describe how selectivity was evaluated and how the sampling plan identified and addressed interferences.
Describe the sensitivity of the technique and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how specificity was evaluated and how the sampling plan addressed it.
Describe how the sampling plan addresses viability assays, if required/applicable, and what the requirements are for maintaining/preserving viability.
Discuss the number of items or the quantity that constitutes an adequate sample for the technique, and whether the samples are composites or grab.
Describe how the sampling technique matches the performance of the applicable analytical assay technique(s).

preconditions.text: If viability measurements are included or if viable organisms are to be detected, then collection and storage techniques may differ from those used to detect nonviable organisms.
Users must develop a safety plan and observe all safety procedures within the plan prior to adopting a sampling technique.

constraints.text: Sampling technique descriptions should list operational limits
A wetted-wall cyclonic sampler may malfunction at temperatures below freezing
Semisolid media are subject to dehydration
Sampling duration may be limited by atmospheric conditions.
Sample flow rate may affect duration.
Holding time and temperature can affect microbiological samples.
Field conditions such as low light, absence of horizontal surfaces, and requirement to wear protective gear may limit technique performance.
Live organisms such as pathogenic microorganisms create potentially high biohazard and infection risks.
Controlling and limiting exposure to health hazards through protective measures (e.g., gloves and glove boxes, particle masks, protective eyewear).
Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) regulations.
for safe handling of chemicals and organisms
Material Safety Data Sheets on file and readily available
